question_text: When a partner is guaranteed a minimum amount of profit, the deficiency (if any) is borne by:
explanation: When a partner is guaranteed a minimum profit, if their actual share falls short, the deficiency is borne by the guaranteeing partner(s). If the guarantee is given by the firm (i.e., by all remaining partners), the deficiency is shared by the other partners in their mutual profit-sharing ratio. If a specific partner gives the guarantee, that partner alone bears the deficiency.
subject: accountancy

options.text: All partners equally including the guaranteed partner

elimination_hints.hint: The guaranteed partner receives the minimum — they don't bear the deficiency. The purpose of a guarantee is to protect them from earning below a threshold.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing who benefits and who pays under a guarantee arrangement.

elimination_hints.hint: The guaranteed partner does not bear any part of the deficiency. Only the guaranteeing partner(s) — typically the other partners — absorb the shortfall.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the guaranteed partner among those who bear the deficiency.

elimination_hints.hint: Creditors have nothing to do with profit distribution among partners. The deficiency is an internal matter between partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing firm's obligations to creditors with inter-partner profit adjustments.

question_text: A, B and C are partners sharing profits in 3:3:2. C is guaranteed a minimum profit of ₹60,000. Any deficiency is to be borne by A and B equally. The firm's profit is ₹2,00,000. The amount of deficiency borne by A is:
explanation: Total profit = ₹2,00,000. C's share = 2,00,000 × 2/8 = ₹50,000. Guaranteed minimum = ₹60,000. Deficiency = 60,000 − 50,000 = ₹10,000. This deficiency is borne by A and B equally: A bears ₹5,000 and B bears ₹5,000. Final shares: A = (2,00,000 × 3/8) − 5,000 = 75,000 − 5,000 = ₹70,000. B = 75,000 − 5,000 = ₹70,000. C = ₹60,000. Total = 2,00,000. ✓
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹10,000 is the TOTAL deficiency (60,000 − 50,000). But it is shared equally between A and B, so each bears 10,000/2 = ₹5,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assigning the entire deficiency to A instead of splitting equally with B.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹6,000 might result from sharing the deficiency in A and B's ratio (3:3 = 1:1, so each gets 5,000 anyway) or a miscalculation. Correct: 10,000/2 = ₹5,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error in computing the deficiency or the sharing.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3,750 might come from sharing the deficiency among A, B and C in 3:3:2 ratio (A's share = 10,000 × 3/8). But C doesn't bear any deficiency — only A and B share it equally.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the guaranteed partner C in the deficiency-sharing calculation.

question_text: X, Y and Z are partners sharing profits in the ratio 5:3:2. Z is guaranteed by X a minimum profit of ₹40,000. The firm's net profit is ₹1,50,000. Z's final share of profit will be:
explanation: Z's actual share = 1,50,000 × 2/10 = ₹30,000. Guaranteed amount = ₹40,000. Deficiency = 40,000 − 30,000 = ₹10,000. Since X alone guarantees Z, X bears the entire deficiency of ₹10,000. Z's final share = ₹40,000 (the guaranteed minimum). X's final share = 1,50,000 × 5/10 − 10,000 = 75,000 − 10,000 = ₹65,000. Y's share remains unchanged = 1,50,000 × 3/10 = ₹45,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹30,000 is Z's ACTUAL share (1,50,000 × 2/10). But Z is guaranteed ₹40,000, so the deficiency of ₹10,000 is paid by X, bringing Z's final share to ₹40,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the guarantee and giving Z only the actual share.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹35,000 doesn't correspond to any correct calculation. Z's actual = 30,000, guaranteed = 40,000, so Z gets ₹40,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Splitting the deficiency when only X is the guarantor.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹45,000 is Y's share (1,50,000 × 3/10), not Z's. Z gets the guaranteed amount of ₹40,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Picking the wrong partner's share.

question_text: When past profits have been wrongly distributed among partners, the adjustment is made through:
explanation: When an error in profit distribution is discovered in subsequent years, the past adjustment is made through a single adjusting journal entry. This entry debits the partner(s) who received excess profit and credits the partner(s) who received less profit. The adjustment is passed through Partners' Capital Accounts (or Current Accounts under fixed capital method) — not through the Profit and Loss Account of the current year, as the error relates to a past period.
subject: accountancy

options.text: An adjusting journal entry through Partners' Capital/Current Accounts
Profit and Loss Account of the current year

elimination_hints.hint: Current year P&L Account should not be used because the error belongs to a prior period. Using current P&L would distort the current year's results.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a past-period adjustment as a current-year income/expense.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation Account is used for revaluing assets and liabilities during reconstitution, not for correcting past profit distribution errors.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing reconstitution-related accounts with profit adjustment entries.

elimination_hints.hint: A memorandum account is not sufficient. Actual journal entries are needed to transfer amounts between partners' accounts to correct the error.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking past adjustments can be handled off-book without formal entries.

question_text: P and Q are partners sharing profits in 3:2. It was later discovered that interest on capital @ 10% was not provided for the last 2 years. Capitals: P = ₹2,00,000, Q = ₹1,00,000. The adjustment entry will involve:
explanation:
Interest on capital not provided for 2 years: P = 2,00,000 × 10% × 2 = ₹40,000. Q = 1,00,000 × 10% × 2 = ₹20,000. Total omitted = ₹60,000.
This ₹60,000 was distributed as profit in 3:2 ratio instead of being first appropriated. P received extra = 60,000 × 3/5 − 40,000 = 36,000 − 40,000 = −₹4,000 (P received ₹4,000 less). Q received extra = 60,000 × 2/5 − 20,000 = 24,000 − 20,000 = ₹4,000 (Q received ₹4,000 more).
Adjustment: Debit Q ₹4,000, Credit P ₹4,000.
subtopic: Adjustment for omitted interest on capital
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the adjustment. P had a larger capital (₹2,00,000) so P was entitled to more interest. Since interest was omitted, P received less than entitled — P should be credited, not debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the debit and credit in the adjustment entry.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹40,000 and ₹20,000 are the total interest amounts, not the net adjustment. The adjustment is the NET difference: who gained and who lost. Net effect = only ₹4,000 transfer needed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using gross interest amounts instead of the net adjustment between partners.

elimination_hints.hint: Same error as C — using gross interest figures. The net adjustment is the difference between what each partner would have received vs what they actually received = ₹4,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that adjustment entries capture only the net difference between partners.

question_text: A, B and C share profits in 2:2:1. Profit for the year was ₹5,00,000. It was later found that A's salary of ₹60,000 p.a. was not charged for the current year. The adjusting entry will be:
explanation:
A's salary omitted = ₹60,000. If salary had been charged: Distributable profit = 5,00,000 − 60,000 = ₹4,40,000.
A should have received: Salary 60,000 + Share (4,40,000 × 2/5) = 60,000 + 1,76,000 = ₹2,36,000.
A actually received: 5,00,000 × 2/5 = ₹2,00,000. A received ₹36,000 less.
B should have received: 4,40,000 × 2/5 = ₹1,76,000. B actually received: 5,00,000 × 2/5 = ₹2,00,000. B received ₹24,000 more.
C should have received: 4,40,000 × 1/5 = ₹88,000. C actually received: 5,00,000 × 1/5 = ₹1,00,000. C received ₹12,000 more.
Adjustment: Debit B ₹24,000, Debit C ₹12,000, Credit A ₹36,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This debits A instead of crediting A. A was the one whose salary was omitted — A should receive (be credited), not pay.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the adjustment — debiting the partner who lost out instead of crediting them.

elimination_hints.hint: B and C don't share equally (30,000 each). Their PSR is 2:1. The ₹60,000 salary not given to A was distributed among all three in 2:2:1. B's excess = 60,000 × 2/5 = ₹24,000, C's excess = 60,000 × 1/5 = ₹12,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Splitting deficiency equally between B and C instead of in their profit-sharing ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹20,000 and ₹16,000 don't match the 2:1 ratio between B and C. Correct: B = 24,000, C = 12,000 (2:1 ratio of the ₹36,000 excess they received).
elimination_hints.misconception: Calculation error in distributing the excess profit among the remaining partners.

question_text: R, S and T are partners sharing profits in 5:3:2. For the last 3 years, profits were distributed equally by mistake. Actual profits were: Year 1: ₹3,00,000, Year 2: ₹4,50,000, Year 3: ₹6,00,000. The net adjustment required is:
explanation:
Total profit over 3 years = 3,00,000 + 4,50,000 + 6,00,000 = ₹13,50,000.
R should have received (5/10): 13,50,000 × 5/10 = ₹6,75,000. R actually received (1/3): 13,50,000 × 1/3 = ₹4,50,000. R received ₹2,25,000 LESS.
S should have received (3/10): 13,50,000 × 3/10 = ₹4,05,000. S actually received: ₹4,50,000. S received ₹45,000 MORE.
T should have received (2/10): 13,50,000 × 2/10 = ₹2,70,000. T actually received: ₹4,50,000. T received ₹1,80,000 MORE.
Adjustment: Debit S ₹45,000, Debit T ₹1,80,000, Credit R ₹2,25,000.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debit S ₹45,000, Debit T ₹1,80,000; Credit R ₹2,25,000
Debit R ₹2,25,000; Credit S ₹45,000, Credit T ₹1,80,000
Debit S ₹1,80,000, Debit T ₹45,000; Credit R ₹2,25,000
Debit S ₹75,000, Debit T ₹1,50,000; Credit R ₹2,25,000

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the adjustment entirely. R had the highest share (5/10) but received only 1/3 — R was underpaid and should be CREDITED, not debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Debiting the partner who received less and crediting those who received more — the exact opposite of correct.

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps the amounts for S and T. S (3/10 share) received ₹45,000 more, while T (2/10 share) received ₹1,80,000 more. Don't mix them up.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing S's and T's excess amounts with each other.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹75,000 and ₹1,50,000 don't match any correct computation. Verify: S excess = 4,50,000 − 4,05,000 = ₹45,000. T excess = 4,50,000 − 2,70,000 = ₹1,80,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error — possibly computing on a per-year basis and making a summation mistake.

question_text: L, M and N are partners sharing profits in 4:3:3. N is guaranteed a minimum profit of ₹80,000 by the firm. The profit for the year is ₹2,00,000. N's actual share and the deficiency are:
explanation: N's actual share = 2,00,000 × 3/10 = ₹60,000. Guaranteed minimum = ₹80,000. Deficiency = 80,000 − 60,000 = ₹20,000. Since the guarantee is by the FIRM, the deficiency is borne by the remaining partners (L and M) in their mutual ratio. L:M mutual ratio = 4:3. L bears = 20,000 × 4/7 = ₹11,429 (approx). M bears = 20,000 × 3/7 = ₹8,571 (approx).
subtopic: Guarantee by firm — deficiency in mutual ratio
subject: accountancy

options.text: Actual share ₹60,000; Deficiency ₹20,000 borne equally by L and M
Actual share ₹60,000; Deficiency ₹20,000 borne by L and M in 4:3
Actual share ₹60,000; Deficiency ₹20,000 borne by L, M and N in 4:3:3

elimination_hints.hint: When the firm gives the guarantee, the deficiency is shared by the remaining partners in their MUTUAL ratio (L:M = 4:3), not equally.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming equal sharing instead of mutual ratio sharing among the remaining partners.

elimination_hints.hint: N's ACTUAL share is 2,00,000 × 3/10 = ₹60,000. The ₹80,000 is the guaranteed minimum, not the actual share. There IS a deficiency of ₹20,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the guaranteed minimum with the actual calculated share.

elimination_hints.hint: N is the guaranteed partner and does NOT bear any part of the deficiency. Only L and M share it in their mutual ratio 4:3.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the guaranteed partner in the deficiency-sharing calculation.

question_text: D, E and F are partners sharing profits equally. The partnership deed provides interest on capital @ 12% p.a. but for the last 2 years, interest was wrongly charged @ 8%. Capitals: D = ₹3,00,000, E = ₹2,00,000, F = ₹1,00,000. The net adjustment entry will involve crediting D by:
explanation:
Shortfall in interest rate = 12% − 8% = 4% per annum, for 2 years.
D's interest shortfall = 3,00,000 × 4% × 2 = ₹24,000.
E's interest shortfall = 2,00,000 × 4% × 2 = ₹16,000.
F's interest shortfall = 1,00,000 × 4% × 2 = ₹8,000.
Total shortfall = ₹48,000. This ₹48,000 was distributed as profit equally (1:1:1).
Each partner received extra from this pool = 48,000/3 = ₹16,000.
D's net position: Should receive ₹24,000 more, but received ₹16,000 extra from profit. Net credit to D = 24,000 − 16,000 = ₹8,000.
E's net position: Should receive ₹16,000 more, received ₹16,000 extra. Net = ₹0.
F's net position: Should receive ₹8,000 more, received ₹16,000 extra. Net debit to F = 16,000 − 8,000 = ₹8,000.
Adjustment: Debit F ₹8,000, Credit D ₹8,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹24,000 is D's total interest shortfall (3,00,000 × 4% × 2). But the adjustment is the NET effect — D also received ₹16,000 extra from profit sharing. Net = 24,000 − 16,000 = ₹8,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using gross shortfall instead of net adjustment after considering the profit redistribution effect.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹16,000 is either E's interest shortfall or each partner's excess profit share. D's net credit = 24,000 − 16,000 = ₹8,000, not ₹16,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing one component (excess profit per partner) with D's net adjustment.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹12,000 doesn't match any calculation path. Verify: D's interest shortfall = ₹24,000, D's extra profit = ₹16,000, Net = ₹8,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Arithmetic error, possibly averaging instead of taking the net difference.

question_text: In case of a guarantee given by a partner to the firm (for minimum profits), the profit of the firm falls short of the guaranteed amount. The guaranteeing partner will:
explanation: When a partner guarantees the firm a minimum profit (e.g., a new partner joining who guarantees to bring minimum revenue), and the firm's actual profit falls short, the guaranteeing partner must compensate the firm for the deficiency. This means the guaranteeing partner's capital account is debited (they pay from their share) and the firm receives the guaranteed minimum.
subtopic: Guarantee given by a partner to the firm
subject: accountancy

options.text: Bear the deficiency from their personal share
Not be affected as the guarantee is void
Share the deficiency with all other partners equally

elimination_hints.hint: A guarantee in a partnership deed is a binding agreement. It cannot be ignored just because profits fell short — that's exactly when it applies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking guarantees are optional or can be voided when needed.

elimination_hints.hint: Only the guaranteeing partner bears the deficiency — that's the nature of a personal guarantee. Other partners are not responsible.
elimination_hints.misconception: Distributing the guarantee burden across all partners instead of the guarantor alone.

elimination_hints.hint: The guaranteeing partner PAYS the deficiency, they don't receive anything. A guarantee is a commitment to cover shortfalls, not a claim for additional profit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the direction of the guarantee obligation.

question_text: A, B and C share profits in 2:2:1. Interest on drawings was charged but not recorded in the books for the last year. Drawings: A = ₹60,000, B = ₹40,000, C = ₹50,000. Rate of interest on drawings = 10% p.a. Average period = 6 months. The net adjustment for A will be:
explanation:
Interest on drawings omitted: A = 60,000 × 10% × 6/12 = ₹3,000. B = 40,000 × 10% × 6/12 = ₹2,000. C = 50,000 × 10% × 6/12 = ₹2,500. Total = ₹7,500.
This ₹7,500 should have been added to distributable profit and shared in 2:2:1.
A should have received from this pool: 7,500 × 2/5 = ₹3,000. A should have paid: ₹3,000.
Net for A = received ₹3,000 − paid ₹3,000 = ₹0. No adjustment needed for A.
B: received 7,500 × 2/5 = ₹3,000, paid ₹2,000. Net credit to B = ₹1,000.
C: received 7,500 × 1/5 = ₹1,500, paid ₹2,500. Net debit to C = ₹1,000.
subtopic: Adjustment for omitted interest on drawings
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3,000 is both A's interest on drawings (debit) AND A's share of profit from the pool (credit). They cancel out exactly. Net = ₹0.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only the credit side (share of profit) without netting off the debit side (interest on drawings).

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3,000 is A's interest on drawings payable, but A also gains ₹3,000 as share of the total interest pool. Net effect = zero.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only the debit side (interest payable) without netting off the credit side.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,000 credit is B's net adjustment, not A's. A's interest paid equals A's share received, resulting in zero adjustment.
elimination_hints.misconception: Picking the wrong partner's adjustment amount.

question_text: When the firm guarantees minimum profit to a partner, the deficiency is borne by the remaining partners in their:
explanation: When the guarantee is given by the firm (not by any specific partner), the deficiency is borne by the remaining partners (excluding the guaranteed partner) in their MUTUAL profit-sharing ratio. Mutual ratio is the ratio between the remaining partners only, excluding the guaranteed partner's share. For example, if A, B and C share 3:2:1 and C is guaranteed, the remaining partners A and B bear deficiency in 3:2.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Old profit-sharing ratio including the guaranteed partner
Mutual profit-sharing ratio (excluding the guaranteed partner)

elimination_hints.hint: The guaranteed partner's ratio is excluded because they don't bear any deficiency — they are the beneficiary. The remaining partners share the burden in their mutual ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Including the guaranteed partner in the deficiency-sharing ratio.

elimination_hints.hint: Equal sharing is the default only when no partnership deed exists. With a deed specifying ratios, deficiency is shared in the mutual PSR of the remaining partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Defaulting to equal sharing regardless of the partnership deed.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital ratio is not used for sharing deficiency unless specifically stated. The default is the mutual profit-sharing ratio of the remaining partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing capital contribution ratio with profit-sharing ratio.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Past adjustments in a partnership are made through a single journal entry without reopening old Profit and Loss Accounts.
Reason (R): Reopening old P&L Accounts would disturb the financial statements of previous years which have already been finalised.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — past adjustments are made through a single adjusting journal entry directly through Partners' Capital/Current Accounts. Old P&L Accounts are not reopened. The Reason is also TRUE — prior year financial statements are already finalised and should not be altered. The Reason correctly explains the Assertion — the single-entry method is used BECAUSE reopening would disturb previously finalised statements.
subtopic: Why single adjusting entry is used
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains WHY past adjustments use a single entry — to avoid disturbing finalised statements. There is a clear causal relationship.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the logical connection between preserving prior statements and using a single adjusting entry.

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason IS true — reopening old accounts would indeed alter finalised financial statements, which is not acceptable accounting practice.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking prior-year P&L accounts can be freely reopened and modified.

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion IS true — past adjustments are universally made through a single journal entry in partnership accounting, not by reopening old accounts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Believing that old P&L accounts should be reopened for corrections.

question_text:
Assertion (A): When a partner's minimum profit guarantee results in zero deficiency, no adjustment entry is needed.
Reason (R): Zero deficiency means the partner's actual share of profit equals or exceeds the guaranteed amount.
Select the correct option:
explanation: The Assertion is TRUE — when there is no deficiency (actual share ≥ guaranteed amount), no adjustment is required because the partner is already receiving at least the guaranteed minimum. The Reason is also TRUE — zero deficiency means the actual profit share is equal to or greater than the guaranteed amount. The Reason correctly explains the Assertion — because the actual share meets the guarantee, no adjustment is needed.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains WHY no entry is needed — because the partner already earns at or above the guarantee. This is a direct cause-effect link.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognizing the logical relationship between no deficiency and no adjustment.

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason IS true — zero deficiency by definition means actual share ≥ guaranteed amount. This is the mathematical meaning of 'no deficiency'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting the definition of zero deficiency.

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion IS true — an adjustment entry is only needed when there is a deficiency (actual < guaranteed). If no deficiency, no entry.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking an entry must always be passed for a guarantee clause regardless of the outcome.

question_text: Past adjustments in partnership accounts require the preparation of a new Profit and Loss Account for the previous year.
explanation: FALSE. Past adjustments do NOT require preparing a new P&L Account. They are corrected through a single adjusting journal entry passed through Partners' Capital/Current Accounts in the current year. The previous year's accounts are not reopened or altered. The adjustment simply transfers the net difference between what each partner received and what they should have received.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: A new P&L Account for the previous year is NEVER prepared. Past adjustments are handled through a single correcting journal entry in partners' accounts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking past errors require restating prior-year financial statements.

question_text: If a partner is guaranteed a minimum profit by a specific partner, the deficiency is shared by all the remaining partners in their profit-sharing ratio.
explanation: FALSE. When a SPECIFIC partner gives the guarantee, ONLY that guaranteeing partner bears the entire deficiency. The other partners are not affected at all. The deficiency is shared by all remaining partners in their mutual ratio only when the guarantee is given by the FIRM (not by any specific individual partner).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: When a SPECIFIC partner guarantees, only THAT partner bears the deficiency. Sharing among remaining partners happens only when the FIRM gives the guarantee.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between guarantee by a specific partner vs guarantee by the firm.

question_text:
Match the following past adjustment scenarios with the correct treatment:

Column I:
(i) Interest on capital omitted
(ii) Partner's salary over-provided
(iii) Interest on drawings not charged
(iv) Profits shared in wrong ratio

Column II:
(P) Credit the partner who received less profit and debit the one who received more
(Q) Debit the partner who received excess salary and credit the others
(R) Credit the partner with higher capital and debit the partner with lower capital (net effect)
(S) Debit the partner with higher drawings and credit the others (net effect)
explanation: (i) Interest on capital omitted → (R): The partner with higher capital was entitled to more interest, so gets credited; partners with lower capital get debited for the net excess they received. (ii) Salary over-provided → (Q): The partner who received excess salary is debited and the others are credited. (iii) Interest on drawings not charged → (S): The partner with higher drawings should have paid more interest, so gets debited; others get credited. (iv) Wrong ratio → (P): The partner who received less than entitled is credited, and the partner who received more is debited.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps (ii) salary over-provided with (iii) interest on drawings. Salary over-provided relates to Q (debit the partner), not S. Interest on drawings relates to S (debit higher-drawing partner), not Q.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up the treatment of salary and interest on drawings adjustments.

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns (i) interest on capital omission to (P) wrong ratio treatment. Interest on capital relates to capital amounts (R), not to wrong profit-sharing ratios (P).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing interest on capital adjustment with wrong ratio adjustment.

elimination_hints.hint: This assigns (i) to drawings treatment (S) and (ii) to capital treatment (R). Interest on capital relates to CAPITAL (R), not drawings (S).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping capital-based and drawings-based adjustment treatments.

question_text: When profits have been distributed in the wrong ratio, the adjustment is made by computing each partner's _______ (excess received or shortfall) and passing a single journal entry.
explanation: The key concept in past adjustments is calculating the NET EFFECT for each partner — the difference between what they SHOULD have received (correct treatment) and what they ACTUALLY received (wrong treatment). Partners who received more than entitled are debited, and those who received less are credited. This 'net effect' or 'net adjustment' is the basis for the single correcting entry.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Gross interest is the total interest amount (e.g., 10% on capital). The adjustment requires the NET difference — what was received vs what should have been received.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using gross amounts instead of net adjustments for correction entries.

elimination_hints.hint: Capital balance is the partner's total capital, not the adjustment amount. Past adjustments calculate the net excess/shortfall in profit distribution, not capital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing capital account balance with the net adjustment for profit errors.

elimination_hints.hint: Sacrificing ratio is used during admission/retirement of partners, not in past profit adjustments. Past adjustments use net effect calculations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Bringing reconstitution concepts into routine past adjustment problems.

question_text: J, K and L are partners sharing profits in 3:2:1. L is guaranteed a minimum profit of ₹1,00,000 per year. Additionally, it is agreed that if the firm's total profit exceeds ₹6,00,000, L will receive 25% of the excess over ₹6,00,000 as bonus. The firm's profit for the year is ₹7,20,000. What is L's total share?
explanation:
Step 1: L's normal share = 7,20,000 × 1/6 = ₹1,20,000.
Step 2: Guaranteed minimum = ₹1,00,000. Since actual share (₹1,20,000) > guaranteed (₹1,00,000), no deficiency.
Step 3: Excess over ₹6,00,000 = 7,20,000 − 6,00,000 = ₹1,20,000.
Step 4: L's bonus = 25% of 1,20,000 = ₹30,000.
Step 5: L's total = Normal share + Bonus = 1,20,000 + 30,000 = ₹1,50,000.
Note: The guarantee doesn't apply here because L's normal share already exceeds the minimum.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,20,000 is L's normal share (7,20,000 × 1/6). But L also earns a bonus of 25% on profit exceeding ₹6,00,000 = 25% of 1,20,000 = ₹30,000. Total = ₹1,50,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the bonus clause that applies when profit exceeds ₹6,00,000.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,30,000 might result from calculating bonus on the wrong base. Bonus = 25% of (7,20,000 − 6,00,000) = 25% of 1,20,000 = ₹30,000, not ₹10,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Miscalculating the excess profit or the bonus percentage.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,00,000 is the guaranteed minimum. But L's actual share (₹1,20,000) exceeds this, and the bonus (₹30,000) adds further. The guarantee is a floor, not a cap.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the guaranteed minimum (floor) with the actual entitlement when profits are high.

question_text:
Arrange the steps for making a past adjustment entry when interest on capital was omitted:
(i) Compute total interest on capital for each partner
(ii) Calculate what each partner would have received after interest
(iii) Compare actual distribution with correct distribution for each partner
(iv) Identify the omission/error
(v) Pass a single journal entry debiting the partner who gained and crediting the partner who lost
explanation: Correct order: (1) Identify the omission/error (iv) — first recognise what went wrong. (2) Compute total interest on capital for each partner (i) — calculate the omitted amounts. (3) Calculate what each partner would have received after interest (ii) — determine correct distribution. (4) Compare actual with correct distribution (iii) — find the net effect for each partner. (5) Pass the adjusting journal entry (v) — debit gainers, credit losers. Order: (iv) → (i) → (ii) → (iii) → (v).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: This computes interest (i) before identifying the error (iv). You must first know WHAT was omitted before calculating amounts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Starting calculations before understanding the nature of the error.

elimination_hints.hint: This passes the journal entry (v) before comparing actual vs correct (iii). You can't record an entry without knowing the amounts to debit/credit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attempting to record an entry before completing the comparison analysis.

elimination_hints.hint: This identifies the error (iv) in the middle. Error identification should be the FIRST step, before any calculations begin.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not prioritising error identification as the starting point of the adjustment process.
